sentences.text: Right.
OK.
When they already had the passages and we were expecting them to come home, we waited.
And it was a long wait without any word.

And every time the doorbell rang-- we were still in our house at that time-- when the doorbell rang, we would-- I would run because I was the quickest of the ladies, and would run to see who was there.
And one day the doorbell rang.
And a man started walking from the ground level up to the-- to the apartment.
I didn't know who it was.
It was Joe, my brother Joe.
He was not wearing his own clothes.
He was wearing clothes that didn't fit, coat that was dragging on the floor.
He had frostbite.
He had turned gray.
He had been in Dachau.
And when Dina and mother stood to receive him at the door, Dina screamed.
She said where's Helmut?
Where's Helmut?
Joe didn't know anything about that Helmut was getting out, why he was getting out.
They just said, get out of here, sent him on home.
So then we waited again another week before Helmut showed up.
And he was in worse condition.
Now, they would sit up at night, the adults, and would talk about their experiences.
And I was sent to bed.
But I got up and listened at the door.
And I listened about the atrocities that occurred, even early on.
They slept on mats.
They were given-- they were hosed down.
They were stripped naked.
There were people snitching on other people.
There was mistrust among the people in the concentration camp.
No one knew what was gonna happen next, where anybody was going.
And rumors and gossips and things like that were going on.
And they talked about the frost bite and who said what and who did what.
I didn't really know what to make of it.
But I did listen and get some glimpses into that.
So Helmut finally came home.
And as I said, his frostbite was worse than Joe's.
And, boy, they were happy to leave.
And, of course, they did.
And that left mother and me behind.
And she carried on to the best of her ability.
I never heard her complain.
We were now on four different continents.
My family was totally separated.
And we would never again live in one continent.
My family was broken up.
From what I hear, and I am not a genealogy buff-- I-- maybe I should be.
I was busy doing other things in my life.
But we probably can go back about 500 years in Germany.
My nephew Len was in Israel.
And he said that he read at an archives there that he thinks that our family came from Spain originally and drifted into Germany.
When we finally were able to leave, the day we left, we only could carry what we could with our hands.
I took my favorite doll and my teddy bear.
And we had a suitcase each, I guess or something.
I always wore a little hat.
And people saw us and told us later what they saw.
They saw later on that first of all Mr.
Poll, that neighbor that bought our building, insisted on helping mother with whatever she had to carry to the train.
And he did.
And I remember saying good bye.
What I didn't know is when the train left, another witness told me later on as an adult that Mr.
Poll was addressed by a Nazi and said, how dare you help these Jews?
I'm gonna jail you.
I'm gonna take you away.
But somehow he made it like a stern, strong warning and he was let go.
But, you see, people did risk their lives.
It was very serious.
What were you allowed to take with you?
Only what we could carry.
No money.
We came here without any money.
And we didn't-- not have-- you know, we had no place to send money ahead of time or hide it anywhere.
We had nothing.
We left everything behind.
Fortunately, when we came here, the lift was here.
We had a little tiny bit of furniture.
Mother had a few clothes.
My clothes didn't fit.
But mother started babysitting in this country as soon as she could learn enough English.
And I went to the school.
Mother was courageous.
She sent me off to a day camp.
And I didn't even speak English yet.
About what?
Ah, that's-- I'm glad you asked.
OK.
We were put-- we had to go to Berlin to get on a train.
And this was a troop train.
And they made two cars for refugees.
Like, they just jam packed them full of refugees.
Jewish refugees, right.
Mother made arrangements through the HIAS Organization by paying them.
The trip was outlined as follows.
We were to go from Germany through occupied France and from occupied France to Spain.
And then in Portugal, we would get our boat to America.
So the trip began, and we were crowded in there.
We had one sink and one toilet per car.
We had to take turns.
HIAS took us off the train perhaps once or twice.
I know we got off in-- in San Sebastian in Spain.
And we were bombarded with Spanish children who were skinnier and more starving than we were begging for our rations that we shared with those little kids.
They were just begging for food.
Spain was ravaged.
However, we were taken to a hotel of some sort.
And all the Jews were fed a meal.
We were able to bathe there.
And I saw lights on again after two years of living in total blackout in Germany.
And no more air raids.
So that was ray of hope.
And then we went to-- on to Portugal.
In-- in Lisbon, we stayed with a family that housed us and fed us.
We stayed there for about a week, during which time I got chicken pox.
I knew later from the scars what it was.
I just had terrible itches and didn't feel well.
So from there, we got on the which was an American ship, all one class.
And it was an OK journey.
We got a little seasick.
I met some nice Americans for the first time.
And, to me, they seemed glamorous and romantic.
And it felt good.
And we had good food.
And then when we landed-- about to land in New York, it was the 4th of July.
And there was so much excitement.
We saw the Statue of Liberty.
And when I-- I think two years later I wrote about it in my school newspaper, that this was the most thrilling experience in my life to that day.
Freedom.
Freedom.
She was our welcome.
When we got off the ship, we didn't know who was going to meet us because it was war years.
And we had no contact.
It was my brother Joe.
He had come from Peru to America, which we knew.
But what we didn't know was that he was in a uniform.
And all uniforms brought fears to us.
What's Joe doing in uniform, Mother said.
And he said he joined the Army.
And Joe served very proudly in the American Army.
He was not sent overseas.
But he was sent to a camp for-- for prisoners of war in Texas.
And he was an interpreter there.
And he-- people said, oh you're-- Josephn Heumann from Leverkusen.
And he totally ignored them.
He didn't want to identify with any of the Germans.
Well, we went live with Alfred and his wife Hildegard who was expecting their first child.
And they lived on Market Road in Chicago, small apartment.
And we all squeezed in.
And we did the best we could.
And we lived with them when my-- my darling nephew was born.
And he was more like a sibling to me because he was closer in age than any of my brothers.
And he was 11 years-- I was 11 when he was born.
And we-- we became close.
And we-- we enjoy each other's families very much to this day.
I helped take care of him.
I was his third mother.
[LAUGHS] First there was his mama, then my-- my mother, his Oma, he adored her.
And then he had me.
So he was raised by three of us.
Well, at first I was lonely for the people I left behind.
And I was worried about them.
I knew that it wasn't good there.
Of course, nobody knew exactly the extent of what was going on.
We, in this country, have to remember that this loss of the freedom of speech is incomprehensible to us when we-- we are privileged to have this freedom.
In Germany, you don't-- you didn't get information.
You didn't-- they fed you what they wanted to have you hear.
We did not know-- people were-- when they-- when they were going to get us, we didn't know they were getting killed.
We knew it wasn't good.
We had a foreboding about it.
But the truth didn't come out till later on.
In the news in this country.
And we thought Roosevelt was our hero and the father of this country and everything else.
I mean, we just worshiped him.
And we didn't know till later that he could have prevented a lot of these things.
He could have bombed the railroad tracks that were leading to the-- to the extermination camps.
He could have done a lot of things that he didn't pay attention to.
And we didn't know that at the time.
My-- my friends today that I went to high school with-- I made-- I was very fortunate.
I made magnificent friends in this country.
I'm still friends with people I knew in grade school that knew me as a little refugee and started not knowing English.
But they helped me along.
And it was the war years.
I didn't want to talk German in any shape or form, even to my mother who had to learn English because I wasn't talking German anymore.
I rebelled.
No more German for me.
That's the end of that.
And I don't want to be different than the kids in this country.
And I'm going to become American.
And so poor Mama had to get used to that.
But she-- she understood that.
In this country, I think-- well, in high school-- in grammar school I had a lot of non-Jewish friends.
But in high school, the Jewish kids hung together and formed a camaraderie.
And-- and I loved it.
And I loved being Jewish and religious.
I asked to go to Sunday school.
We couldn't afford to belong to a temple.
But they did afford to send me to Sunday school.
And I never missed a Sunday because I loved going.
And I was confirmed.
Kids weren't bat mitzvahed at that time-- girls.
But I enjoyed every moment of it.
I enjoyed my religious freedom.

Later when I had children, I would cry that they could partake, when they were the age I was, that they could freely attend synagogues here and had the privilege of belonging and enjoying their religion.
I just-- It meant so much to me.
Appreciated that freedom.
I really did.
And I've always been affiliated.
And I'm a strong believer that we must support our temples.
Well, the Holocaust is-- is too hard to explain.
And I think we have to reshape our religion.
And we are a strong peoplehood.
How can I explain that?
I still have some faith because I need the faith that there is somehow a God.
And I would still pray to that God.
I don't know if it's a man or woman.
In my temple, we have gender-free services once a month, which I think are wonderful.
But the important thing is to have some kind of faith to survive.
And people have done this from the early civilization.
They've had to.
No.
It has not strengthened my belief.
It has strengthened my feeling for the Jews as a people and that we need each other and that we have to help each other no matter where we are in the world.
The peoplehood, to me, is-- is the most important thing.
Yes.
Yes, it does.

Mother, as I told you, I think I mentioned earlier, that she-- the experiences in World War I, the loss of her husband, raising the children by herself, she was an independent woman, she-- a woman of strong faith.
She had such faith that she went to visit Helmut when she was in this country, who lived in Peru, and she had no money.
She had to save up what little she had.
I had to work as soon as I could to support myself and give her a little money.
And Helmut also sent her whatever he could each month so that eventually we could move out of Alfred's home so he and Hildegard and their children could have privacy.
And we got a little apartment that cost us something like $45.
I think the most we ever paid was $65 a month over a tavern, a two-room office space that Alfred created into living quarters for us.
He had a lot of ingenuity, was very artistic.
And we had a toilet down the hall that was like in a closet.
And we had a rubber bath tub.
But we managed.
And mother went on and served her good meals.
And she was a lady.
And she had pride.
And she lived like it was a palace.
And we had freedom.
And she made it home for you.
She made home.
So she decided to visit Helmut.
And she took a ship.
And it took three weeks.
And we said when she came back, Mom, what took so long?
She said, well, I dined with the captain every night.
But we went slow because the cargo was nitroglycerin.
This is the kind of ship she went on?
That's right.
She sailed with the captain.
[LAUGHS] She was determined to see her son.
Yes.
And later on she took-- made a trip to Israel.
When Helmut left Peru and moved to Israel, she visited him there and his two boys so that she-- they could know her.
My future husband was a World War II hero, I think.
I met him at a party, which was a specific party from the B'nai B'rith young women.
And he was invited.
And sparks flew the first time we saw each other.
It wasn't long till we started dating.
And we married one year to the date that we met.
His name is Earl Arkiss.
I was 21 when I met him, 22 when I married him.
Our life together went very well.
He was a struggling young lawyer.
And I was a legal secretary.
He didn't want me to work for lawyers.
He knew how hard they were to work for.
But I had a series of jobs.
Now, I did experience prejudice in those jobs.
When I worked for Greyhound Post Houses, I was the only Jewish person.
And it was a large office.
I had an Irish boss.
And I said to him-- I had two bosses.
And they-- I sat in the middle of the two of them.
One was the chief engineer of the company.
The other one was the purchasing agent.
And I sat in the middle.
And I said one day, Mr.
Stensin, I want to take off for the Jewish holidays next week.
And he says, you what?
You, you, you, you're Jewish?
And I said, yes.
He stormed out and went to the personnel office to check that out.
It was in my records.
In those days, they asked.
And he was a little bit upset.
And he said to Mr.
Goty, he says, Chuck, Chuck, Chuck did you know she was Jewish?
And Chuck said, so what?
And that was that.
Now, a week later, I get a tap on the shoulder.
Miss Heumann?
Yes, sir?
The president of the company, said-- Mr.
Brown-- said I want to see you in my office right now.
So I went into his office.
And he said Miss Heumann, I want you to go to work for me.
And you're gonna get a raise.
[LAUGHS]
Again, you have the good and the bad.
You have it here just like you did in Europe.
There are prejudiced, ugly people all over the world.
Ignorant people, evil people.
And then I worked there a little longer until they moved out.
They moved to Berwyn, and I couldn't go that far.
So I left that company.
And I worked-- went to work for a publishing firm.
And again, at-- at a lunch hour, I said to my coworkers, I won't be here next week.
I have a Jewish holiday.
And this young woman I was friends with, she said, you better not tell anyone you're Jewish.
I burst into tears.
I just-- I was a refugee.
It hurt me so deeply, you know?
So another woman that was present reported this incident to the personnel woman.
Personnel lady came to me and said, Ruth, you have nothing to worry about.
Ignore all that.
But it was there.
It was there.
But there was a difference from Germany to here.
Oh, yeah.
Yeah.
Although my husband experienced tremendous prejudice.
A job that he applied for they didn't hire him.
And they told him because he was a Jew.
And he said, thank you for being honest about it.
But it wasn't sanctioned by the government here.
No.
But it did take place here.
Yeah.
And I do have to say that my husband and I had one enormous difference, which is our background.
He grew up in Chicago.
He-- in his neighborhood they have the Jewish Defense League, OK.
If anybody insulted you, you hit.
And I have a refugee mentality.
He does not.
He was born in freedom.
And he fought.
And he'd give somebody a bloody nose.
And if he didn't do it, his brother would.
And he won't tolerate it, OK.
But I'm a shrinking violet.
I'm still very sensitive.
Of course.
Well, the children came, I think, we were married three years.
And we planned to have a family.
And Leslie came and she was a joy.
And--
She was born at Michael Reese hospital in Chicago.
And then we plan to have another child.
And that took me awhile because I had several miscarriages.
And then Gary came three years later.
And we had a little boy and we were thrilled again.
And we had what they call a 'rich man's family'-- one of each.
You mean the people I left behind--
Somehow, I-- we-- we-- my mother had correspondence from Germany.
And Hans Egan's mother would write to her, and-- and then through a rumor.
And then pictures were sent to us of our-- the homes that my father built.
And I have pictures of how they were originally.
And there were many bombings of Leverkusen because it was a target area.
Bayer Company, IG Farben was an ammunitions factory.
And that's why the English came there so often to bomb.
And-- OK.
Revulsion, horror, and I can't-- what-- what I can tell you is that I would read the Leon Uris book-- what was it-- And I was sick for a week afterwards because it could have been me.
It was incomprehensible.
I read Elie Wiesel's book, And it-- it stuck with me.
It has.
And I can't-- I can't see those films about the Holocaust.
I can't.
If I just hear the music on a little piece and-- on the television, I get sick to my stomach.
Well, I have-- you mean my background and my culture-- culturally?
Sure.
There was a big influence.
With being associated with the Nazis?
Well, I was a Jewish German.
And you know, that's different.
It's like an existential thing you're thrown into whatever is given you in life.
And this was me.
My husband's background is Russian.
And he brought rich things from that culture through his Jewish mother, which I did through my Jewish mother.
She had marvelous values and things she taught me.
We just happened to live in that country.
We were-- I mean, after all, the Jews were thrown from one country to another.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] So we're survivors.
We do what we can.
You know.
I don't know how to explain that.
It definitely had effects on me.
I can't say it didn't.
Well, I know that I was fearful, protective, you know?
I did the best I could as a mother.
I really-- I think that also a part of being German is being somewhat perfectionistic and doing everything right.
But no one can.
You know.
And I see things now I would do different possibly.
But I did the best I could.
And I did get to go back to school when my kids were grow-- grown.
Almost through high school I entered college.
And I went to school for 10 years.
I became a professional counselor and a mediator.
I had a wonderful career.
And I'm thankful for everything that I have.
And today my husband and I volunteer and help the homeless and work in a homeless shelter occasionally.
That's through our temple.
Yes.
We were thrown out of our home.
We lost everything.
Loss is very hard.
Loss is very, very hard and scary.
Well, he's probably my protector.
He's-- I think when we were asked to come back to Germany, I almost tore the letter up, threw it in the garbage can.
That was my first instinct.
And I put it aside and didn't even answer it till I heard from my brothers.
Alfred said, let's go.
We'll--
The invitation from-- was from Oberburgermeister of-- of the town of Leverkusen, who is now dead.
He was a younger man than I am.
His-- he had many reasons that he listed for inviting the Jews back.
He said, this is your town.
I want to welcome you back.
We want to bridge the past to the future.
Today's people are not the people that caused what happened then.
We all suffered.
Let's get together.
Welcome back home.
Come visit-- visit each other, see each other again.
We were in seven countries, the people from Leverkusen.
And we gathered there.
As I said, I didn't want to go at first.
His letter, however, was very poetic and sincere, beautiful.
OK.
There was nothing in there that turned me off.
In fact, the second letter listed all the people and where they were coming from.
And I thought, my god, it's worthwhile just to see them and especially for my brothers, who were adults, to-- to meet with their friends once more.
OK.
The other thing is we had a mission to identify Dad's grave and get a new headstone on there for him.
OK.
So my brother Alfred said, let's go.
Helmut's willing to come.
We'll have our own reunion.
We can't afford to travel.
Let's go.
They're inviting us, and they're paying for it.
So we decided-- They-- they paid for your air travel?
They paid for the whole trip for one week.
Yes.
And it was the best thing I could have done in retrospect because it gave me such closure.
And I learned so much more about myself that I could never identify.
